content: Establishing teratogenicity requires: consistent pattern of defects (specific syndrome), temporal relationship (exposure during critical period), biological plausibility, dose-response relationship, reproducibility across studies. Single case reports insufficient; registry data and cohort studies needed. Confounding factors: underlying maternal disease may cause defects (diabetes, epilepsy); recall bias in retrospective studies; healthy user bias. Evaluation of exposed pregnancy: detailed ultrasound (18-20 weeks anatomy scan), fetal echocardiography for cardiac teratogens, amniocentesis if indicated. Postnatal assessment: physical examination, developmental monitoring. Causality assessment tools: WHO-UMC criteria, Naranjo scale adapted for pregnancy. Registry follow-up essential for new drugs; pregnancy exposure registries mandated for some medications.
